content: Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) is atherosclerotic occlusion of lower extremity arteries, affecting ~8.5 million Americans ≥40 years. Pathophysiology identical to coronary/cerebrovascular atherosclerosis—endothelial dysfunction, plaque formation, stenosis/occlusion. Risk factors: smoking (strongest association), diabetes (increases risk 2-4x, accelerates progression), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, age, CKD. Clinical presentations: asymptomatic (most common), intermittent claudication (exertional leg pain relieved by rest), atypical ischemic leg pain, critical limb ischemia (rest pain, tissue loss, gangrene), acute limb ischemia (sudden onset—embolism or thrombosis). Functional impairment significant even in asymptomatic patients. PAD is marker for systemic atherosclerosis—high risk of MI, stroke, CV death. Ankle-brachial index (ABI): ratio of systolic BP at ankle to arm; normal 1.00-1.40, borderline 0.91-0.99, abnormal ≤0.90 (diagnostic), noncompressible >1.40 (calcified vessels—common in diabetes, CKD).

PAD diagnosis: resting ABI first-line test. Exercise ABI if resting normal but high suspicion (drop >20% post-exercise diagnostic). Segmental pressures and pulse volume recordings localize disease level. Duplex ultrasound assesses stenosis severity, graft surveillance. CTA or MRA for procedural planning (anatomic detail, calcification assessment). Catheter angiography reserved for intervention. Treatment: cardiovascular risk reduction paramount (statins, antiplatelet therapy, BP control, smoking cessation, diabetes management). Antiplatelet therapy: aspirin 75-100mg daily or clopidogrel 75mg daily; COMPASS trial showed rivaroxaban 2.5mg BID plus aspirin superior to aspirin alone for CV and limb events in symptomatic PAD. Claudication management: supervised exercise therapy (SET) most effective—30-45 minutes treadmill walking 3x/week for 12 weeks improves walking distance; cilostazol 100mg BID contraindicated in heart failure (improves walking distance, mechanism PDE3 inhibition); pentoxifylline less effective. Revascularization indications: disabling claudication despite medical/exercise therapy, critical limb ischemia, acute limb ischemia.

Revascularization for PAD: endovascular first-line for most (angioplasty, stenting, atherectomy, drug-coated balloons)—lower perioperative morbidity than surgery. Surgical bypass (autologous vein preferred) for long-segment disease, failed endovascular, or critical limb ischemia with good surgical candidates. Critical limb ischemia (CLI): rest pain >2 weeks, tissue loss, ABI <0.40, ankle pressure <50 mmHg—requires urgent revascularization to prevent amputation. Acute limb ischemia: 6 Ps (pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, poikilothermia); Rutherford classification guides urgency; immediate anticoagulation (heparin), emergent revascularization (catheter-directed thrombolysis, thrombectomy, bypass). Amputation indicated for nonsalvageable limb, nonambulatory patients, severe comorbidities precluding revascularization. Prognosis: claudication has benign limb prognosis (major amputation <5% at 5 years) but high CV mortality (~30% at 5 years); CLI has poor prognosis without revascularization (amputation rate 25-40% at 1 year, mortality 20-25%). Wound care, infection control, offloading essential for tissue loss.
